title: Allergic Rhinitis
oneLiner: Common IgE-mediated inflammation of nasal mucosa triggered by allergens (pollen, dust mites, pets); impacts quality of life and exacerbates asthma.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Anaphylaxis** – sudden onset urticaria/angioedema, stridor, wheeze, hypotension, GI symptoms within minutes of allergen exposure (e.g., venom, food, latex). **Key finding:** respiratory distress + hypotension + skin/mucosal changes. Treat immediately with IM adrenaline 0.3–0.5mg (lateral thigh) before any other tests.
- **Acute severe asthma exacerbation** – allergic rhinitis is a common trigger; look for silent chest, inability to speak, use of accessory muscles, exhaustion, SpO₂<94%. **Key finding:** peak expiratory flow <33% predicted AND/OR rising pCO₂ on VBG. Call for senior help and start back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium nebs + IV hydrocortisone 200mg.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** persistent/recurrent sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea, nasal congestion, itchy nose/eyes/palate, postnasal drip. Often seasonal (intermittent) or perennial (persistent). More than 4 days/week or >4 weeks if persistent.
- **Key exam findings:** pale, bluish/purple oedematous nasal mucosa; clear thin secretions; "allergic shiners" (dark circles under eyes); "allergic salute" (transverse nasal crease); conjunctival injection; cobblestoning of pharyngeal wall; reduced nasal airflow.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral rhinitis (common cold)** – fever, purulent nasal discharge, myalgia; duration <10 days; no pruritus.
- **Non-allergic rhinitis (e.g., vasomotor, drug-induced)** – negative skin prick/sIgE; triggers are irritants, temperature, exercise, medications (e.g., alpha-blockers, cocaine). **Splitter:** itching is absent in non-allergic rhinitis.
- **Chronic rhinosinusitis with/without nasal polyps** – mucopurulent discharge, facial pain/pressure, hyposmia/anosmia; nasal endoscopy shows polyps or oedema.
- **Rhinitis medicamentosa** – recent prolonged use of topical decongestants (e.g., oxymetazoline, xylometazoline >5 days).
- **CSF rhinorrhoea** – clear, watery, salty taste; glucose-positive dipstick; unilateral; associated with head trauma/meningitis history.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** history of typical triggers and seasonal pattern is usually diagnostic. Anterior rhinoscopy (with otoscope if no nasal speculum) – look for mucosal colour, polyps, discharge.
- **Bloods:** Not routinely needed. Consider full blood count (eosinophilia), total IgE, allergen-specific IgE (ImmunoCAP) if skin prick testing unavailable or on antihistamines that can't be stopped.
- **Imaging:** None for uncomplicated allergic rhinitis. CT sinuses only if suspect chronic rhinosinusitis or structural abnormality (e.g., polyps, sinusitis).
- **Special tests:** Skin prick testing (gold standard) – stop antihistamines 7 days prior; positive wheal ≥3mm larger than negative control. Nasal provocation testing (rarely needed).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** ARIA classification – Intermittent (<4 days/week or <4 weeks) vs Persistent (≥4 days/week and ≥4 weeks); plus severity by impact on sleep/daily activities (mild/moderate-severe).
- **Never-miss test:** Assessment of asthma control (peak flow, spirometry, ACT questionnaire) – allergic rhinitis is often comorbid; untreated rhinitis worsens asthma outcomes.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Only if severe allergic reaction or acute asthma – treat those presentations first (see Killers). For isolated rhinitis, no acute hospital treatment needed; advise avoidance measures (e.g., stay indoors in high pollen, mite-proof covers, wash bedding >60°C). Oral antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine 10mg stat) if itching and sneezing prominent.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Not typically admitted. If seen as part of asthma admission, ensure intranasal corticosteroid started (e.g., fluticasone propionate 50mcg/spray, 2 sprays each nostril BD) plus regular oral antihistamine (e.g., loratadine 10mg daily). Discharge planning with GP follow-up.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Step 1 (mild intermittent):** Intranasal antihistamine (e.g., azelastine 137mcg/spray, 1–2 sprays each nostril BD) or oral antihistamine daily.
  - **Step 2 (persistent/moderate-severe):** First-line intranasal corticosteroid (e.g., fluticasone furoate 110mcg/spray, 2 sprays each nostril OD). Add oral antihistamine if still symptomatic after 2 weeks.
  - **Step 3 (refractory):** Consider leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast 10mg nocte) – especially in asthmatics. Add topical antihistamine (olopatadine) or ipratropium bromide spray for rhinorrhoea.
  - **Step 4 (severe, uncontrolled):** Refer to allergy/specialist for allergen immunotherapy (SLIT/SCIT) – consider in those with severe symptoms failing drug therapy, or who want desensitisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Rarely required. If comorbidity (e.g., asthma exacerbation) – O₂ to target SpO₂ 94–98%, regular observations 4-hourly, IV access if severe.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Intranasal corticosteroid** – fluticasone furoate (Avamys®) 110mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril once daily (total 220mcg/day). Alternative: mometasone furoate (Nasonex®) 50mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril OD-BD.
  - **Oral antihistamine** – loratadine 10mg OD, or cetirizine 10mg OD, or fexofenadine 180mg OD (non-sedating).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Intranasal antihistamine – azelastine (137mcg/spray): 1–2 sprays each nostril BD.
  - Combined intranasal fluticasone/azelastine (Dymista®): 1 spray each nostril BD.
  - Montelukast 10mg OD for nocturnal symptoms or in asthmatics.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Montelukast caution in neuropsychiatric events (e.g., agitation, depression); advise patients to stop if mood changes.
  - Intranasal steroids: rare septum perforation with incorrect technique; avoid in active nasal herpes or prior nasal surgery unless specialist.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No significant dose adjustment for intranasal steroids; oral antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine) reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <10ml/min – avoid). Montelukast no renal adjustment; caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Using non-sedating antihistamines at doses exceeding licensed maximum (e.g., fexofenadine >180mg daily)** can increase sedation and cardiotoxicity risk (QTc prolongation especially with cetirizine/loratadine at high doses). Also, **prescribing oral decongestants (pseudoephedrine) in patients with hypertension, IHD, or hyperthyroidism** – can cause hypertensive crisis/arrhythmia.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Acute sinusitis (bacterial superinfection), acute otitis media (Eustachian tube dysfunction), sleep disturbance, impaired school/work performance.
- **Chronic:** Nasal polyps, chronic sinusitis, olfactory dysfunction, asthma exacerbation, impaired quality of life, dental malocclusion from mouth breathing in children.
sections.Prognosis: - Allergic rhinitis is a chronic condition but symptoms can be well-controlled with regular medication and allergen avoidance. No mortality directly, but complications (asthma attacks, sinusitis) can cause morbidity. Spontaneous remission occurs in ~10-20% over years; severity often reduces with age.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always assess asthma status** in any patient with allergic rhinitis – use the 3-item RCP questions or ACT score. If uncontrolled, refer for asthma review.
- **Teach proper intranasal spray technique:** blow nose first, tilt head forward, aim spray laterally away from septum, avoid sniffing immediately after.
- **Step-up therapy if no response in 2 weeks** – do not continue an ineffective dose of intranasal steroid; increase frequency or add second-line agent.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe oral decongestants (pseudoephedrine) for >5 days** – risk of rhinitis medicamentosa.
- **Do not rule out allergy based on negative skin prick test alone** – non-allergic rhinitis is common, and false negatives occur (especially if antihistamines were not stopped).
- **Do not delay referral for immunotherapy** in patients with severe, uncontrolled symptoms despite maximum medical therapy – it can reduce symptom burden and prevent asthma progression.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Severe facial pain/fever/purulent nasal discharge (signs of sinusitis needing antibiotics). Shortness of breath, wheeze, or asthma symptoms worsening despite treatment. New-onset nasal bleeding or crusting (suspect septal perforation – stop intranasal steroid and seek ENT). Anaphylaxis symptoms (lip/tongue swelling, difficulty breathing, collapse) – use own adrenaline auto-injector or call 999.
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2–4 weeks to review symptom control and medication adjustment. If on immunotherapy, follow local allergy clinic schedule. Refer to ENT/Allergy specialist if symptoms persist after 3–6 months of step 3 treatment.
sections.Escalation Criteria: - **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:** Any acute asthma exacerbation that fails to improve after 3 back-to-back nebs with IV hydrocortisone – PEF <50% predicted, SpO₂ <92% on O₂, rising PaCO₂, or GCS <15. Signs of anaphylaxis (hypotension, stridor, widespread urticaria) – trigger anaphylaxis protocol and call arrest team if no response to IM adrenaline within 5 minutes. **NEWS2 threshold:** ≥5 or any single parameter of 3 (e.g., BP <90/60, RR >30, SpO₂ <91% without O₂). **Lactate:** >4 mmol/L indicates hypoperfusion/shock. **GCS:** ≤12 consider PICU/ICU referral.

rawMarkdown:
## Allergic Rhinitis
**One-liner:** Common IgE-mediated inflammation of nasal mucosa triggered by allergens (pollen, dust mites, pets); impacts quality of life and exacerbates asthma.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Anaphylaxis** – sudden onset urticaria/angioedema, stridor, wheeze, hypotension, GI symptoms within minutes of allergen exposure (e.g., venom, food, latex). **Key finding:** respiratory distress + hypotension + skin/mucosal changes. Treat immediately with IM adrenaline 0.3–0.5mg (lateral thigh) before any other tests.
- **Acute severe asthma exacerbation** – allergic rhinitis is a common trigger; look for silent chest, inability to speak, use of accessory muscles, exhaustion, SpO₂<94%. **Key finding:** peak expiratory flow <33% predicted AND/OR rising pCO₂ on VBG. Call for senior help and start back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium nebs + IV hydrocortisone 200mg.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** persistent/recurrent sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea, nasal congestion, itchy nose/eyes/palate, postnasal drip. Often seasonal (intermittent) or perennial (persistent). More than 4 days/week or >4 weeks if persistent.
- **Key exam findings:** pale, bluish/purple oedematous nasal mucosa; clear thin secretions; "allergic shiners" (dark circles under eyes); "allergic salute" (transverse nasal crease); conjunctival injection; cobblestoning of pharyngeal wall; reduced nasal airflow.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral rhinitis (common cold)** – fever, purulent nasal discharge, myalgia; duration <10 days; no pruritus.
- **Non-allergic rhinitis (e.g., vasomotor, drug-induced)** – negative skin prick/sIgE; triggers are irritants, temperature, exercise, medications (e.g., alpha-blockers, cocaine). **Splitter:** itching is absent in non-allergic rhinitis.
- **Chronic rhinosinusitis with/without nasal polyps** – mucopurulent discharge, facial pain/pressure, hyposmia/anosmia; nasal endoscopy shows polyps or oedema.
- **Rhinitis medicamentosa** – recent prolonged use of topical decongestants (e.g., oxymetazoline, xylometazoline >5 days).
- **CSF rhinorrhoea** – clear, watery, salty taste; glucose-positive dipstick; unilateral; associated with head trauma/meningitis history.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** history of typical triggers and seasonal pattern is usually diagnostic. Anterior rhinoscopy (with otoscope if no nasal speculum) – look for mucosal colour, polyps, discharge.
- **Bloods:** Not routinely needed. Consider full blood count (eosinophilia), total IgE, allergen-specific IgE (ImmunoCAP) if skin prick testing unavailable or on antihistamines that can't be stopped.
- **Imaging:** None for uncomplicated allergic rhinitis. CT sinuses only if suspect chronic rhinosinusitis or structural abnormality (e.g., polyps, sinusitis).
- **Special tests:** Skin prick testing (gold standard) – stop antihistamines 7 days prior; positive wheal ≥3mm larger than negative control. Nasal provocation testing (rarely needed).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** ARIA classification – Intermittent (<4 days/week or <4 weeks) vs Persistent (≥4 days/week and ≥4 weeks); plus severity by impact on sleep/daily activities (mild/moderate-severe).
- **Never-miss test:** Assessment of asthma control (peak flow, spirometry, ACT questionnaire) – allergic rhinitis is often comorbid; untreated rhinitis worsens asthma outcomes.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Only if severe allergic reaction or acute asthma – treat those presentations first (see Killers). For isolated rhinitis, no acute hospital treatment needed; advise avoidance measures (e.g., stay indoors in high pollen, mite-proof covers, wash bedding >60°C). Oral antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine 10mg stat) if itching and sneezing prominent.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Not typically admitted. If seen as part of asthma admission, ensure intranasal corticosteroid started (e.g., fluticasone propionate 50mcg/spray, 2 sprays each nostril BD) plus regular oral antihistamine (e.g., loratadine 10mg daily). Discharge planning with GP follow-up.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Step 1 (mild intermittent):** Intranasal antihistamine (e.g., azelastine 137mcg/spray, 1–2 sprays each nostril BD) or oral antihistamine daily.
  - **Step 2 (persistent/moderate-severe):** First-line intranasal corticosteroid (e.g., fluticasone furoate 110mcg/spray, 2 sprays each nostril OD). Add oral antihistamine if still symptomatic after 2 weeks.
  - **Step 3 (refractory):** Consider leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast 10mg nocte) – especially in asthmatics. Add topical antihistamine (olopatadine) or ipratropium bromide spray for rhinorrhoea.
  - **Step 4 (severe, uncontrolled):** Refer to allergy/specialist for allergen immunotherapy (SLIT/SCIT) – consider in those with severe symptoms failing drug therapy, or who want desensitisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Rarely required. If comorbidity (e.g., asthma exacerbation) – O₂ to target SpO₂ 94–98%, regular observations 4-hourly, IV access if severe.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Intranasal corticosteroid** – fluticasone furoate (Avamys®) 110mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril once daily (total 220mcg/day). Alternative: mometasone furoate (Nasonex®) 50mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril OD-BD.
  - **Oral antihistamine** – loratadine 10mg OD, or cetirizine 10mg OD, or fexofenadine 180mg OD (non-sedating).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Intranasal antihistamine – azelastine (137mcg/spray): 1–2 sprays each nostril BD.
  - Combined intranasal fluticasone/azelastine (Dymista®): 1 spray each nostril BD.
  - Montelukast 10mg OD for nocturnal symptoms or in asthmatics.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Montelukast caution in neuropsychiatric events (e.g., agitation, depression); advise patients to stop if mood changes.
  - Intranasal steroids: rare septum perforation with incorrect technique; avoid in active nasal herpes or prior nasal surgery unless specialist.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No significant dose adjustment for intranasal steroids; oral antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine) reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <10ml/min – avoid). Montelukast no renal adjustment; caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Using non-sedating antihistamines at doses exceeding licensed maximum (e.g., fexofenadine >180mg daily)** can increase sedation and cardiotoxicity risk (QTc prolongation especially with cetirizine/loratadine at high doses). Also, **prescribing oral decongestants (pseudoephedrine) in patients with hypertension, IHD, or hyperthyroidism** – can cause hypertensive crisis/arrhythmia.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Acute sinusitis (bacterial superinfection), acute otitis media (Eustachian tube dysfunction), sleep disturbance, impaired school/work performance.
- **Chronic:** Nasal polyps, chronic sinusitis, olfactory dysfunction, asthma exacerbation, impaired quality of life, dental malocclusion from mouth breathing in children.

### Prognosis
- Allergic rhinitis is a chronic condition but symptoms can be well-controlled with regular medication and allergen avoidance. No mortality directly, but complications (asthma attacks, sinusitis) can cause morbidity. Spontaneous remission occurs in ~10-20% over years; severity often reduces with age.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always assess asthma status** in any patient with allergic rhinitis – use the 3-item RCP questions or ACT score. If uncontrolled, refer for asthma review.
- **Teach proper intranasal spray technique:** blow nose first, tilt head forward, aim spray laterally away from septum, avoid sniffing immediately after.
- **Step-up therapy if no response in 2 weeks** – do not continue an ineffective dose of intranasal steroid; increase frequency or add second-line agent.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe oral decongestants (pseudoephedrine) for >5 days** – risk of rhinitis medicamentosa.
- **Do not rule out allergy based on negative skin prick test alone** – non-allergic rhinitis is common, and false negatives occur (especially if antihistamines were not stopped).
- **Do not delay referral for immunotherapy** in patients with severe, uncontrolled symptoms despite maximum medical therapy – it can reduce symptom burden and prevent asthma progression.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Severe facial pain/fever/purulent nasal discharge (signs of sinusitis needing antibiotics). Shortness of breath, wheeze, or asthma symptoms worsening despite treatment. New-onset nasal bleeding or crusting (suspect septal perforation – stop intranasal steroid and seek ENT). Anaphylaxis symptoms (lip/tongue swelling, difficulty breathing, collapse) – use own adrenaline auto-injector or call 999.
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2–4 weeks to review symptom control and medication adjustment. If on immunotherapy, follow local allergy clinic schedule. Refer to ENT/Allergy specialist if symptoms persist after 3–6 months of step 3 treatment.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:** Any acute asthma exacerbation that fails to improve after 3 back-to-back nebs with IV hydrocortisone – PEF <50% predicted, SpO₂ <92% on O₂, rising PaCO₂, or GCS <15. Signs of anaphylaxis (hypotension, stridor, widespread urticaria) – trigger anaphylaxis protocol and call arrest team if no response to IM adrenaline within 5 minutes. **NEWS2 threshold:** ≥5 or any single parameter of 3 (e.g., BP <90/60, RR >30, SpO₂ <91% without O₂). **Lactate:** >4 mmol/L indicates hypoperfusion/shock. **GCS:** ≤12 consider PICU/ICU referral.
